message:
test(ext-conformance): add TS↔Rust parity runner with scenario execution (bd-vmm)

Create run_scenario.ts (TS oracle for tool/command/event execution) and
parity_runner test that runs the same scenarios in both TS (Bun + jiti)
and Rust (QuickJS), normalizes outputs, and diffs them. Produces
per-scenario and per-extension JSONL logs with parity triage report.

Results: 4 match, 0 mismatch across executable scenarios (permission-gate,
todo, hello, with-deps). 1 known env-dependent diff (subagent agent
discovery), 18 skipped (unsupported setup features).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2lr3","title":"Workstream: Property-based + fuzz testing for core parsers","description":"# Goal
Add property-based and fuzz testing to harden the core “parser/serializer” surfaces where small edge cases can cause correctness or security issues.

# Why
Even with strong unit + conformance coverage, these areas are classic footguns:
- streaming parsers (SSE)
- truncation logic (line/byte budgets)
- JSONL session parsing/serialization
- path normalization and globbing

Property/fuzz tests are an *additional* safety net and a way to systematically discover cases we didn’t think of.

# Scope
- Introduce a small, well-scoped proptest suite for a few high-leverage invariants.
- Optionally add `cargo-fuzz` harnesses if we can keep them reproducible and non-flaky.

# Constraints
- Must not require network.
- Must not introduce brittle golden tests.
- Keep runtime bounded (CI friendly).

# Definition of Done
- We have repeatable generators/invariants for at least SSE + truncation + session JSONL.
- Any discovered edge cases are added to deterministic unit/conformance tests.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

 {"id":"bd-2m6d","title":"Research: OpenClaw/ClawHub marketplace inventory","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:10:43.762438974Z","created_by":"LavenderRobin","updated_at":"2026-02-05T08:08:01.287994878Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","marketplace","openclaw","research"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2m6d","depends_on_id":"bd-19rf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:20:44.825240797Z","created_by":"LavenderRobin"},{"issue_id":"bd-2m6d","depends_on_id":"bd-2hap","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-05T08:07:36.795526478Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2m6d","depends_on_id":"bd-d7gn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:10:45.986507999Z","created_by":"LavenderRobin"}],"comments":[{"id":295,"issue_id":"bd-2m6d","author":"LavenderRobin","text":"BACKGROUND
- OpenClaw-style marketplaces can instantly dwarf the legacy pi-mono extension set. If OpenClaw integrates Pi Agent (or shares compatible extension formats), it becomes a primary “popularity” signal and a major source of real-world extensions.

Task goal
- Produce a reproducible, machine-readable inventory of marketplace-listed extensions/skills that are plausibly Pi extensions.

Method (must be repeatable)
1) Identify canonical sources
- Find the official OpenClaw GitHub org/repo and any official marketplace/hub (often a website + API).
- Prefer official JSON/GraphQL endpoints or repo-hosted indexes over scraping.

2) Export raw marketplace index
- Capture the full listing (IDs, names, source URLs, categories/tags, ranking/installs/downloads if available).
- Save the raw dump + timestamp so the inventory can be regenerated and audited.

3) Filter to “true Pi extension” candidates
- Validate candidates by *code signature* where possible (repo contains TS/JS entrypoints consistent with Pi extension protocol).
- If the marketplace mixes unrelated items (prompts, MCP servers, templates), keep them in a “non-extension” bucket but do not count them as Pi extensions.

4) Emit structured candidate records
- For each validated candidate, emit minimum fields needed by bd-hhzv:
  - source_tier=openclaw (or analogous)
  - upstream URL(s)
  - popularity evidence (rank/installs/stars)
  - any packaging cues (repo vs npm vs hosted)

Acceptance criteria
- Document the exact endpoints/queries used + how many items were retrieved.
- Provide a candidate list for the “openclaw” tier that can be fed into the master catalog pipeline.
- Explicitly state one of:
  - “OpenClaw extensions are Pi-protocol-compatible” (with proof), OR
  - “OpenClaw uses a different extension API” (with a compatibility mapping plan and what can/cannot be reused).

Downstream
- Blocks bd-hhzv (candidate inventory) and any OpenClaw-specific corpus tiering.
","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:12:31Z"},{"id":364,"issue_id":"bd-2m6d","author":"LavenderRobin","text":"TESTING + LOGGING REQUIREMENTS

Even though this is “research”, we should treat the marketplace inventory as a reproducible data pipeline.

Required artifacts
- Raw marketplace dumps (prefer JSON/API responses) saved with timestamps.
- A normalized, machine-readable candidate list (stable ordering + stable IDs).

Unit tests (parser/normalizer)
- Store at least 2 fixture snapshots from the marketplace index (small + large) and write unit tests that:
  - parse raw marketplace payloads into candidate records
  - validate stable IDs + ordering
  - ensure “non-extension” items are classified and excluded from the Pi-extension counts

E2E script
- Provide an offline E2E script that:
  - runs the OpenClaw inventory pipeline against fixtures
  - produces the normalized output
  - asserts expected counts + key fields

Logging
- Emit JSONL logs with:
  - query/endpoints used + HTTP status
  - item counts (raw/filtered/validated)
  - per-item classification decisions (true-extension vs out-of-scope) with reasons
  - timing (so we can enforce “super fast” discovery refresh)

Security
- Redact tokens/secrets from logs; record only non-sensitive provenance.

 {"id":"bd-2nng","title":"Enable syntax highlighting in rich_rust for code blocks","description":"Enable syntax highlighting for code blocks in tool output and assistant responses.

## Current State
- rich_rust has 'syntax' feature available but not enabled
- Code blocks from tools (read, bash, grep) show without highlighting
- glamour has optional syntax-highlighting feature also unused

## Implementation
1. Enable 'syntax' feature in Cargo.toml for rich_rust
2. Create helper in PiConsole to detect language and apply highlighting
3. Use Syntax renderable for code output in tool results
4. Integrate with existing truncation logic

## Binary Size Impact
- Adds ~2MB (syntect with embedded syntax definitions)
- Consider: Could use glamour's syntax-highlighting instead (shared dep)

## Language Detection
- File extension mapping (rs->rust, py->python, etc.)
- Shebang detection for scripts
- Fallback to 'text' for unknown

## Test Plan
- Unit test: syntax highlighting produces colored ANSI for known languages
- Manual test: read tool output for .rs file shows Rust syntax colors

## Files to Modify
- Cargo.toml (add syntax feature)
- src/tui.rs (add syntax highlighting method)
- src/tools.rs (use highlighting in read/grep output)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:08:46.125262433Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T22:16:35.126638605Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T22:16:35.126576650Z","close_reason":"PiConsole markdown: syntax-highlight fenced code blocks via rich_rust::Syntax + unit test","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2nng","depends_on_id":"bd-20lm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:08:46.135805889Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-vs72","title":"Implement pi.model control APIs (setModel, getThinkingLevel, setThinkingLevel)","description":"Allow extensions to control model and thinking level.

## API Spec (from legacy)
```typescript
await pi.setModel(model: Model): Promise<boolean>
pi.getThinkingLevel(): ThinkingLevel  // 'off'|'minimal'|'low'|'medium'|'high'|'xhigh'
pi.setThinkingLevel(level: ThinkingLevel): void
```

## Use Cases
- Auto-switching models based on task complexity
- Preset systems (different models for different workflows)
- Dynamic thinking level adjustment

## Implementation Requirements
1. Hostcall handlers for all three APIs
2. setModel is async (may need provider initialization)
3. Thinking level clamped to model capabilities
4. Model change creates ModelChangeEntry in session

## Behavior Notes
- setModel returns false if model not available
- setThinkingLevel clamped to model's max thinking
- Changes affect next agent turn

## Test Plan
- Unit test: setModel changes current model
- Unit test: getThinkingLevel returns current level
- Unit test: setThinkingLevel clamped correctly
- Integration test: model change persisted to session

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions.rs (hostcall handlers)
- src/agent.rs (model/thinking management)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:11:31.602941272Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T04:40:07.991999788Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T04:40:07.991936610Z","close_reason":"All four model control APIs (getModel, setModel, getThinkingLevel, setThinkingLevel) are fully implemented: JS bridge functions in extensions_js.rs (lines 2929-2946), pi object exports (lines 3464-3467), Rust hostcall handlers in extensions.rs (lines 5828-5901) with session persistence via ExtensionSession trait, and ExtensionManager backing state.
